_meta.game: Castle of Illusion Starring Mickey Mouse
notes: Castle of Illusion is Sega's flagship platformer for the Genesis launch era — widely regarded as one of the best Genesis platformers.
Mickey Mouse game with polished, fluid physics distinct from the NES era.
COMBAT MECHANIC: Bounce-stomp (jump on enemy from above). Mickey bounces back up off the enemy.
PROJECTILE MECHANIC: Mickey can carry apples, marbles, and stars and throw them horizontally.
Mickey cannot harm enemies from the side — must bounce from above or throw projectiles.
Limited projectiles — must find them in the level.

walk_speed.notes: Approximate from gameplay. Mickey feels fluid and responsive — faster than NES-era standards. VERIFY.

run_speed.notes: Mickey doesn't have a separate run — consistent movement speed.

jump.notes: Full air control, variable height. The jump feel is lighter and more floaty than Mega Man but more controlled than Mario's early NES games.

bounce_jump.description: When Mickey lands on an enemy from above (butt-stomp or downward jump), he bounces back up to jump height.
notes: The bounce restores full jump height — enemies are essentially trampolines.
Chaining bounces off multiple enemies maintains momentum and height.
This is mechanically similar to Mario's stomp but the bounce-back height is more generous.

butt_stomp.description: Primary attack: jump above an enemy and land on them from above. Defeats the enemy and bounces Mickey upward.
notes: Standard traversal combat in platformers from this era, but the bounce-back is particularly generous in CofI.
Cannot harm enemies from the side — the hitbox only activates from above.
This forces players to always approach from above, creating aerial puzzle-solving.

projectile_throw.description: Mickey can pick up apples, marbles, or stars from the environment and throw them horizontally.
notes: Projectile supply is limited — found in specific spots in the level.
Projectiles hit enemies Mickey can't reach with the stomp (some enemies require standing attacks).
Different projectile types may have different speeds or damage. VERIFY.

gem_collecting.description: Mickey collects gems throughout stages. 100 gems = extra life.
gem_collecting.notes: Classic platformer collectible bonus life system.

world_themes.boss_levels: Each world ends with a boss fight
world_themes.notes: The castle interior is divided into themed wings — each wing is a different themed world Mickey must traverse to free Minnie.

mickey_standing.notes: Mickey's hitbox is friendly to the player — narrower than the sprite. VERIFY exact dimensions.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: Castle of Illusion's 'approach from above' combat requirement forces the player to always think 3-dimensionally: 'where am I relative to this enemy's head?' This is different from Mario (stomp is casual) because in CofI you MUST achieve the height advantage or switch to limited projectiles.
The bounce-back mechanic from enemy stomps makes enemy encounters into aerial opportunities — skilled players maintain momentum by chaining stomps. Enemies become platforms AND obstacles.

The themed world design (forest → toys → storm → library → candy) shows how varied visual themes can use the same physics engine. Giants Drink's world generation should similarly be able to apply different aesthetic themes to the same physics.

The limited projectile supply creates resource scarcity in combat. Players must decide when projectiles are worth using — this creates tactical decision-making in what would otherwise be purely reactive combat.
CofI represents the Genesis's early best-foot-forward: fluid animation, responsive controls, consistent frame rate, varied visual themes. It established what Genesis platformers could aspire to.
